nodes.name: Nobel Prizes in Science
nodes.description: Notable Nobel Prize winners in Physics, Chemistry, and Physiology/Medicine

items.prompt: Which German-born physicist won the 1921 Nobel Prize in Physics — not for his theories of relativity, which the committee considered too speculative, but for explaining the photoelectric effect by proposing that light is made of discrete energy packets called photons?
items.explanation:
The Nobel citation read: 'for his services to Theoretical Physics, and especially for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect.'
Relativity was excluded — Einstein received the prize a year late (awarded in 1922 for 1921).
Einstein donated the prize money (~32,000 Swedish kronor) to his first wife Mileva Marić per their divorce agreement.
At the time, Einstein was already the most famous scientist in the world following Eddington's 1919 eclipse confirmation of general relativity.

items.prompt: Who is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences — Physics in 1903 (shared with Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel) for research on radioactivity, and Chemistry in 1911 for the discovery of radium and polonium?
items.explanation:
First woman to win a Nobel Prize (1903 Physics); her 1911 Chemistry prize made her the only person to win in two different sciences.
She coined the term 'radioactivity.'
The French Academy of Sciences refused to admit her despite her two Nobel Prizes.
Her personal notebooks remain radioactive to this day and are stored in lead-lined boxes at the Bibliothèque nationale de France.
items.cardFront: Only Nobel winner in two different sciences (Physics 1903 + Chemistry 1911)

items.prompt: Which New Zealand-born physicist, called the 'father of nuclear physics,' won the 1908 Nobel Prize in Chemistry — not Physics — for discovering that radioactivity involves the transmutation of one element into another; he famously quipped about the irony of his overnight transformation into a chemist?
items.explanation:
Nobel awarded 'for his investigations into the disintegration of the elements and the chemistry of radioactive substances.'
Rutherford identified and named alpha and beta radiation; later (1911) discovered the atomic nucleus via the gold foil experiment.
Joked: 'I have dealt with many transformations in my time but never so quick as my transformation into a chemist.'
Element 104, rutherfordium (Rf), is named in his honor.
Many of his students and colleagues also won Nobel Prizes — he is sometimes called the 'crocodile' for his relentless drive.

items.prompt: What discovery was the basis of the 1945 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, shared by Alexander Fleming, Howard Florey, and Ernst Boris Chain — Fleming for the accidental 1928 observation, Florey and Chain for developing it into a usable drug?
items.explanation:
Fleming discovered penicillin in September 1928 when Penicillium mold contaminated and killed bacteria on a petri dish.
Florey and Chain at Oxford purified and mass-produced penicillin (1940–41); battlefield use began in WWII.
Penicillin is estimated to have saved over 200 million lives.
Fleming warned in his Nobel lecture about antibiotic resistance — a problem now globally recognized.

items.prompt: What was the basis of the 1962 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine awarded to Francis Crick, James Watson, and Maurice Wilkins — controversially omitting Rosalind Franklin, who had died in 1958 and whose X-ray image Photo 51 was central to the discovery?
alternates: the double helix structure of DNA
items.explanation:
Watson and Crick's one-page *Nature* paper (April 1953) described the double helix structure of DNA.
Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51 X-ray diffraction image was shown to Watson by Wilkins without her knowledge.
Franklin died of ovarian cancer in 1958 at 37; Nobel Prizes cannot be awarded posthumously.
Crick and Watson's paper famously ended: 'It has not escaped our notice that the specific pairing we have postulated immediately suggests a possible copying mechanism for the genetic material.'

items.prompt: Which American chemist is the only person to have won two unshared Nobel Prizes — Chemistry in 1954 for work on the nature of the chemical bond and protein structures, and the Nobel Peace Prize in 1962 for his campaign against nuclear weapons testing?
items.explanation:
Won the 1954 Chemistry Nobel unshared for his work on electronegativity, covalent bonds, and the alpha helix and beta sheet structures of proteins.
Won the 1962 Peace Nobel unshared for circulating the Mainau Declaration against nuclear weapons and lobbying for the Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty.
The only person to win two solo (unshared) Nobel Prizes; Marie Curie won in two different sciences but shared the Physics prize.
During McCarthyism, his passport was revoked over alleged communist sympathies; it was restored just in time to accept the 1954 Nobel.
items.cardFront: Only person to win two unshared Nobel Prizes (Chemistry 1954 + Peace 1962)

items.prompt: What 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier for developing a method of genome editing — using a bacterial immune-system mechanism as 'molecular scissors' that can cut DNA at any location in any organism?
items.explanation:
Doudna (UC Berkeley) and Charpentier (Max Planck Institute) published the foundational CRISPR-Cas9 paper in *Science* in June 2012.
First two women to share a Nobel Prize in Chemistry without a male co-recipient.
CRISPR: Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats — sequences bacteria use to recognize and destroy returning viruses.
Applications: treating sickle-cell disease, developing cancer therapies, engineering disease-resistant crops.

items.prompt: What 2013 Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded to Peter Higgs and François Englert for their 1964 theoretical prediction of a field that gives fundamental particles their mass — confirmed experimentally at CERN's Large Hadron Collider in 2012?
items.explanation:
The Higgs field permeates all of space; particles acquire mass by interacting with it.
The associated particle (the Higgs boson) was detected on July 4, 2012, by the ATLAS and CMS experiments at the LHC.
Robert Brout, who co-authored the original 1964 paper with Englert, died in 2011 and was not eligible for the posthumous award.
The particle is nicknamed the 'God particle' — a label Peter Higgs reportedly found 'embarrassing' and 'inappropriate.'
items.cardFront: Physics 2013 — particles acquire mass via the Higgs field

items.prompt: What 2017 Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded to Rainer Weiss, Barry Barish, and Kip Thorne for the detection of gravitational waves — ripples in spacetime predicted by Einstein in 1916 and first directly observed on September 14, 2015, by the LIGO collaboration?
items.explanation:
The first detection (GW150914) measured displacement of ~10⁻¹⁸ m — 1/1,000 the diameter of a proton — from two black holes merging 1.3 billion light-years away.
LIGO: Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory; twin detectors in Hanford, WA, and Livingston, LA.
Weiss (MIT) designed the laser interferometer concept; Barish and Thorne (Caltech) built and led the collaboration.
The 2017 neutron star merger (GW170817) was the first event detected in both gravitational waves and electromagnetic light — launching multi-messenger astronomy.

items.prompt: What 2024 Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded to John Hopfield and Geoffrey Hinton for foundational discoveries that enabled modern machine learning — Hopfield for inventing an associative memory network, and Hinton for developing the backpropagation algorithm for training artificial neural networks?
items.explanation:
John Hopfield (Princeton) invented the Hopfield network in 1982: a recurrent neural network that stores patterns by minimizing an energy function inspired by statistical physics.
Geoffrey Hinton (Toronto/Google) developed backpropagation, dropout, and deep learning techniques that underpin modern AI.
Hinton resigned from Google in 2023, citing concerns about AI safety and wanting to speak freely about AI risks.
Controversially awarded to Physics — the Nobel Committee emphasized the physics principles (statistical mechanics, spin glasses) underlying the mathematical framework.
items.cardFront: Physics 2024 — artificial neural networks and machine learning

items.prompt: What 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was shared by David Baker, Demis Hassabis, and John Jumper — Baker for computational protein design, Hassabis and Jumper for AlphaFold, the AI system that solved the 50-year-old problem of predicting a protein's 3D structure from its amino acid sequence?
items.answer: Computational protein design and protein structure prediction
items.explanation:
AlphaFold (Google DeepMind) predicts a protein's 3D structure from its amino acid sequence with near-experimental accuracy in hours or minutes.
Before AlphaFold, determining a structure by X-ray crystallography could take years; the 'protein folding problem' had been open since 1969.
AlphaFold has predicted structures for ~200 million proteins — virtually the entire known protein universe — released free to researchers.
Demis Hassabis is CEO of Google DeepMind; John Jumper led the AlphaFold team; David Baker (Univ. of Washington) pioneered de novo computational protein design.
items.cardFront: Chemistry 2024 — AlphaFold solves the protein-folding problem

items.prompt: What 2024 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded to Victor Ambros and Gary Ruvkun for discovering microRNA — small RNA molecules that regulate gene expression after transcription and are found in all multicellular organisms?
items.explanation:
Ambros (UMass Medical) and Ruvkun (Harvard/MGH) discovered the first microRNA, *lin-4*, in the roundworm *C. elegans* in 1993.
Initially dismissed as a worm curiosity; later found in all multicellular organisms, including humans (~2,000 human microRNAs).
MicroRNAs (~22 nucleotides) bind complementary sequences in mRNA and block translation or trigger mRNA degradation.
Dysregulation of microRNAs is associated with cancer, heart disease, and neurological disorders — now a target for drug development.
items.cardFront: Medicine 2024 — small RNA molecules regulate gene expression

items.prompt: What 2025 Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded to John Clarke, Michel Devoret, and John Martinis for demonstrating that macroscopic electrical circuits (specifically Josephson junctions) exhibit quantum mechanical behavior — specifically energy quantization and quantum tunneling — foundational for quantum computing?
items.answer: Macroscopic quantum tunneling in electrical circuits
items.explanation:
Josephson junctions are superconducting circuits that exhibit quantum mechanical effects at macroscopic (visible) scales — energy is quantized just as in atoms.
This work underpins the design of superconducting qubits used in current quantum computers by IBM, Google, and others.
John Martinis (UC Santa Barbara/Google) led the team that claimed 'quantum supremacy' in 2019 with Google's Sycamore chip.
Michel Devoret (Yale) and John Clarke (UC Berkeley) developed the theoretical and experimental foundations in the 1980s and 1990s.
items.cardFront: Physics 2025 — quantum mechanics in superconducting electrical circuits

items.prompt: What 2025 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to Susumu Kitagawa, Richard Robson, and Omar Yaghi for developing metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) — highly porous crystalline structures with extraordinary internal surface areas, used for gas storage, carbon capture, and water harvesting?
items.explanation:
MOFs are crystalline networks of metal ions or clusters linked by organic molecules, creating cage-like structures with internal surface areas up to 10,000 m²/g — more than a football field in a teaspoon of material.
Applications: hydrogen storage for fuel cells, capturing CO2 from air, harvesting drinking water from desert air, and drug delivery.
Omar Yaghi (UC Berkeley) synthesized MOF-5 in 1999 with record porosity; Susumu Kitagawa (Kyoto) developed flexible 'soft' porous MOFs.
Over 100,000 distinct MOF structures have been synthesized and characterized since the 1990s.
items.cardFront: Chemistry 2025 — ultra-porous crystalline materials for gas storage and capture

items.prompt: What 2025 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded to Mary Brunkow, Frederick Ramsdell, and Shimon Sakaguchi for discovering peripheral immune tolerance — specifically the regulatory T cells (Tregs) controlled by the FOXP3 gene that prevent the immune system from attacking the body's own tissues?
items.explanation:
Sakaguchi (Osaka University) demonstrated in 1995 that removing CD25⁺ T cells from mice caused widespread autoimmune disease — identifying Tregs as essential self-tolerance suppressors.
FOXP3 is the master transcription factor controlling Treg identity; mutations in FOXP3 cause IPEX syndrome (severe autoimmunity and immune dysregulation) in humans.
Brunkow and Ramsdell identified FOXP3 as the gene mutated in the 'scurfy' mouse, an autoimmune mouse model.
Understanding Tregs opened new therapies: removing Tregs in cancer to unleash immune attacks on tumors (checkpoint inhibitors); boosting Tregs to treat autoimmune diseases and prevent transplant rejection.
items.cardFront: Medicine 2025 — immune cells that prevent autoimmune disease
